facebook.content_angle: SWOLF score explained — why stroke efficiency is the overlooked metric NZ competitive swimmers should be tracking, tied to NAGS 2026 race week
facebook.post_text:
Most swimmers obsess over lap times. The best ones obsess over their **SWOLF score**. 🔢

If you're putting in the training but not seeing the times drop, stroke efficiency could be what's holding you back.

SWOLF = your stroke count per length + your time for that length. Lower is better. It tells you whether you're swimming smarter, not just harder.

Here's how to test it right now:
👉 Swim one length at race pace, count every stroke
👉 Add your stroke count to your time in seconds
👉 That's your SWOLF — now work to reduce it each session

Here's the part most swimmers miss 😳

Your suit matters more than you think. Every drag point adds strokes. A lower-drag competition suit lets your body glide further per stroke — which directly drops your SWOLF.

✔️ Longer glide phase = fewer strokes needed
✔️ Fewer strokes = lower SWOLF = faster times
✔️ Jaked suits are engineered precisely for this — less drag, more distance per stroke

With **NAGS 2026** running in Hastings this week, there's no better time to get serious about the details that actually move the needle.

Are you tracking your SWOLF? 👇
facebook.engagement_bait: Drop your SWOLF score below if you've ever tested it — or tag a training partner who needs to start! 🏊‍♂️
